description: When no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients is configured and the client MAC is not learned then the first L2VPN DHCP client packet is dropped.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for DHCP Relay?
Feature Caveat: DHCP Relay - When no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients is configured and the client MAC is not learned then the first L2VPN DHCP client packet is dropped.

description: When configuring the same BGP neighbor under both IPv4 and IPv6 address families on a router, if a maximum prefix limit is set on one address family (like IPv4), exceeding that limit can potentially impact the BGP session for the other address family (IPv6) as well.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for BGP?

Feature Caveat: BGP - When configuring the same BGP neighbor under both IPv4 and IPv6 address families on a router, if a maximum prefix limit is set on one address family (like IPv4), exceeding that limit can potentially impact the BGP session for the other address family (IPv6) as well.

description: If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for Activate?

Feature Caveat: Activate - If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

description: The next-hop-unchanged option needs to be explicitly configured to preserve nexthop while advertising routes to eBGP peers, in the L2VPN EVPN address-family. For example: Copy router bgp

messages.content: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what limitation is mentioned for BGP?

Feature Caveat: BGP - The next-hop-unchanged option needs to be explicitly configured to preserve nexthop while advertising routes to eBGP peers, in the L2VPN EVPN address-family. For example: Copy router bgp.

description: Symptom: Ethernet-tag mappings are permitted only when the operational state of the EVPN VLAN-aware bundle is disabled. Scenario: In previous versions of AOS-CX, modifications to VLAN: Ethernet-tag mappings were permitted only when the operational state of the EVPN VLAN-aware bundle was disabled. In AOS-CX 10.17, this restriction has been relaxed-modifications are now allowed regardless of the operational state, provided that the resulting effective Ethernet tag does not conflict with any existing VLAN tag assignment. This ensures configuration flexibility while maintaining tag uniqueness and preventing forwarding conflicts.

description: Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the swith, switch #1's port 1/1/2 will be assigned the identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two 6200, 6300, 6300L, 6400, 8100, or 8360 switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or don't use both of the ports with the overlap. Neither of those options is a feasible solution for a customer. Prior to the fix provided here, there is no explicit workaround for this issue.

description: Symptom: When wireless clients roam between access points connected to the same switch, they lose connectivity and receive an APIPA address. Connectivity is only restored after the MAC-age timer expires. Scenario: When a client is authenticated with a VLAN on a secure port, the VLAN is added as an ingress-only VLAN on other secure ports, but the MSTP state for those ports remains in blocked state instead of forwarding state, preventing seamless client movement. Workaround: Reduce the MAC-age timer to minimize the impact. or configure AP ports in device-mode instead of client-mode: interface 1/1/X port-access client-limit 1 port-access client-mode device.

description: Symptom: Traffic loss in an SVI with vsx active-forwarding enabled, when vsx active-forwarding is disabled/removed on a entirely different SVI. Scenario: In a configuration where vsx active-forwarding is enabled, a special HW resource must be programmed for the VSX peers to handle traffic destined to the active-gateway IP. The trigger for this programming is tied to our router MAC programming (packets destined to this MAC that ingress on an L3 interface will perform an L3 lookup), and the ref counting for these router MACs had a bug. Since the ref counting would decrement early, we would remove our special HW resource and traffic loss can occur depending on which peer it lands on. Workaround: Unconfigure and reconfigure vsx active-forwarding in the SVI, or save the config and reboot the VSX peers

description: Symptom: A drop occurs when packet(s) match an applied policy with PBR default-nexthop action and the LPM/longest prefix matching route in the VRF for that packet is a route type that copies to CPU (unresolved, nullroute, reject or interface). Scenario: This issue can occur in the following workflows: A policy with PBR action is applied to a Layer 3 interface in a given VRF in the routed-in direction and the selected/active entry from the PBR action list is of type default-nexthop. A packet ingresses the system on the same Layer 3 interface and the packet is matched by the qualifiers in the policy's IPv4 or IPv6 class. There exists one or more matching routes for the packet's destination address in the VRF and the longest prefix matching route (LPM) of that set is one of the following route types: + unresolved - the route's configured IPv4/IPv6 nexthop is currently unreachable + interface - the route is configured to egress matching traffic directly to another Layer 3 interface in the VRF, e.g. ROP, VLAN SVI or L3 LAG + nullroute - the route is configured to discard packets and return ICMP error to the sender + reject - the route is configured to discard packets silently This situation could occur randomly as dynamic routing topology changes introduce or remove routes of varying prefix lengths and nexthop states and/or the highest priority entry in the PBR action list changes type (e.g. default-nexthop to nexthop) depending on the changing reachability of the list entries. Workaround: Avoid using the PBR default-nexthop action entirely and use nexthop action instead, taking into account the fact that nexthop has higher priority and will redirect over any routing table entries in the VRF where the default-nexthop action has priority over the VRF's default route only, if one exists.

There exists one or more matching routes for the packet's destination address in the VRF and the longest prefix matching route (LPM) of that set is one of the following route types: + unresolved - the route's configured IPv4/IPv6 nexthop is currently unreachable + interface - the route is configured to egress matching traffic directly to another Layer 3 interface in the VRF, e.g. ROP, VLAN SVI or L3 LAG + nullroute - the route is configured to discard packets and return ICMP error to the sender + reject - the route is configured to discard packets silently This situation could occur randomly as dynamic routing topology changes introduce or remove routes of varying prefix lengths and nexthop states and/or the highest priority entry in the PBR action list changes type (e.g. default-nexthop to nexthop) depending on the changing reachability of the list entries. Workaround: Avoid using the PBR default-nexthop action entirely and use nexthop action instead, taking into account the fact that nexthop has higher priority and will redirect over any routing table entries in the VRF where the default-nexthop action has priority over the VRF's default route only, if one exists.

description: Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the system, Switch #1's port 1/1/2 is assigned an identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.
